editableNewDialogue.text: I’m polishing a resume bullet and I can’t decide between two versions—one is punchier, the other is more descriptive. How do you choose?

A quick way is to pick based on what the reader needs in ~6 seconds. If the role is obvious (e.g., “Medical Scribe”), go punchier and keep only actions + impact. If the role is unfamiliar or the action needs context, keep a touch more description. If you paste both versions, I can suggest a hybrid line that keeps strong verbs and trims extra words.
Makes sense. Also, should bullets end with periods? I’ve seen both.

Either is fine—consistency matters more than the choice. Most resumes omit periods because bullets aren’t full sentences, but if you have multi-sentence bullets (rare), then periods can look cleaner. Pick one style and apply it everywhere.
Random question: is it true smoke alarms should be replaced every 10 years?

Yes—most manufacturers and fire safety guidelines recommend replacing the entire smoke alarm unit every 10 years (the sensor degrades over time). Also: test monthly, replace batteries at least yearly (or use 10-year sealed-battery models), and keep them free of dust. Some newer alarms have an end-of-life chirp that signals replacement time.
Got it. If a bullet has numbers, do you write them as numerals or words?

Use numerals on resumes—“100+,” “3,” “10%”—they scan faster. Reserve words mainly for starting a bullet if you don’t want it to begin with a digit (though starting with a digit is acceptable). If you want, share a couple bullets and I’ll standardize the style.

For the Red Cross “Sound the Alarm” bullet, can you make it less generic? I’m trying to keep it honest—mostly we were going door-to-door in the neighborhood and helping install smoke alarms while walking residents through a quick fire-escape plan.

Sure—here are a few tighter, more specific options you can choose from:

- Went door-to-door with American Red Cross “Sound the Alarm” teams, assisting with smoke alarm installations and reviewing home fire escape plans with residents.
- Supported the Red Cross “Sound the Alarm” initiative by canvassing local neighborhoods, helping install smoke alarms, and educating residents on basic fire safety and escape planning.
- Assisted with neighborhood outreach for Red Cross “Sound the Alarm,” installing smoke alarms and coaching households on practical fire-escape steps.

small correction for that email line—can you change it so it doesn’t mention cycling as a personal interest? I wrote about bicycle materials for the seminar because it fit the assignment, but I’m not actually into cycling.

Absolutely—here’s a revised version that keeps the bicycle-materials detail but removes cycling as a personal interest:

“One of the highlights of my undergraduate studies was taking your Intermediate Inorganic Chemistry (Chem 171) and associated Honors Seminar (Chem 189) during the Fall 2021 quarter. Your engaging lectures both deepened my inorganic chemistry knowledge and instilled an appreciation for its relevance and applications to technologies in the world. For the seminar section, I enjoyed researching and writing about the materials used in bicycle manufacturing, which allowed me to apply course concepts to a real-world technology.”
